meaning: To endure a painful or otherwise unpleasant situation that is seen as unavoidable.
literalMeaning: To literally bite down on a bullet.
origin: Historically, patients would bite on a bullet to cope with the pain of surgery before anesthetics were common.
examplesJson: ["I hate going to the dentist, but I'll just have to bite the bullet.", "Deciding it was time to bite the bullet, he finally paid off his debts."]

meaning: To do or say something to relieve tension or get conversation going at the start of a party or when people meet for the first time.
origin: In the days of sail-powered exploration, special ships called 'icebreakers' would forge ahead to create a path for others.
examplesJson: ["He told a joke to break the ice at the start of the meeting.", "It can be hard to break the ice with strangers."]

meaning: Slightly unwell or in low spirits.
origin: A nautical term. When a sailor was unwell, they would be sent below deck to protect them from the weather.
examplesJson: ["I'm feeling a bit under the weather today.", "She called in sick because she was under the weather."]

meaning: To reveal secret information unintentionally or indiscreetly.
origin: Possibly derived from an ancient Greek method of voting using white and black beans in a jar. If the jar tipped over, the secret vote was revealed.
examplesJson: ["Come on, spill the beans! What did he say?", "We're organizing a surprise party, so don't spill the beans."]
similarIdiomsJson: ["Let the cat out of the bag", "Give the game away"]

meaning: An apparent misfortune that eventually has good results.
literalMeaning: A blessing that is wearing a disguise.
origin: First appeared in print in the mid-18th century, used to describe an event that initially seems bad but turns out to be beneficial.
examplesJson: ["Losing that job was a blessing in disguise; it pushed me to start my own business.", "The rain on our wedding day was a blessing in disguise because it forced us inside where it was warmer."]
